title: Application hit RDS max_connections during traffic spike
symptoms: Application logs show 'FATAL: too many connections for role app'
RDS DatabaseConnections metric is flat at the maximum
Users see 5xx responses during peak hours
root_cause: Each pod opens its own connection pool and traffic doubled
Long-lived Lambda concurrency spikes created new connections faster than RDS could recycle
No connection pooler (RDS Proxy or PgBouncer) in front of the database
diagnosis: Check Performance Insights → Top SQL for hanging sessions
SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state;
Look at CloudWatch DatabaseConnections and CPUUtilization
fix: Introduce RDS Proxy in front of the database:

```bash
aws rds create-db-proxy \
  --db-proxy-name app-proxy \
  --engine-family POSTGRESQL \
  --auth AuthScheme=SECRETS,SecretArn=$SECRET \
  --role-arn $ROLE_ARN
```
Lower the application pool size per pod and raise RDS max_connections in the parameter group
Kill stuck sessions: `SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'idle in transaction' AND state_change < now() - interval '10 min';`
prevention: Load test the pool size × replica count against max_connections
Adopt RDS Proxy for all serverless workloads
Alert at 70% of max_connections

title: Application receives 403 AccessDenied when reading S3 after IAM change
symptoms: boto3 or aws s3 cp returns 403 AccessDenied
Worked fine before the last IAM policy update
CloudTrail shows errorCode AccessDenied
root_cause: Overly restrictive bucket policy conflicts with IAM role
Missing kms:Decrypt permission for the SSE-KMS key
S3 Block Public Access or VPC endpoint policy blocking the call
diagnosis: Copy the CloudTrail entry and paste into the IAM policy simulator
Check bucket policy: `aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <name>`
Inspect the VPC endpoint policy if using PrivateLink
fix: Grant the role permission to the KMS key:

```json
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": ["kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey"],
  "Resource": "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123:key/abc"
}
```
Align the bucket policy principal ARN with the actual role used at runtime
Re-deploy with the fresh credentials and validate via `aws sts get-caller-identity`
prevention: Enforce a break-glass review for bucket policy changes
Use Access Analyzer to detect unintended public access
Write integration tests that read/write a canary object on deploy

title: Lambda functions experiencing high cold start latency during traffic spikes
symptoms: API Gateway 504 timeouts during initial requests after idle period
CloudWatch logs show initialization times > 5 seconds
Users report slow first-page load experience
Cold start rate spikes correlate with deployment events
root_cause: Lambda functions not provisioned with Provisioned Concurrency
Heavy initialization logic including large dependency bundles
VPC configuration adds network setup overhead during cold starts
Memory size too low causing slower initialization
diagnosis: Check CloudWatch Metrics → Lambda → Duration and InitDuration
Enable X-Ray tracing to visualize cold start patterns
Review function package size: `aws lambda get-function --function-name <name>`
Analyze traffic patterns to identify peak periods
fix: Enable Provisioned Concurrency for critical functions:

```bash
aws lambda put-provisioned-concurrency-config \
  --function-name my-function \
  --provisioned-concurrent-executions 10
```
Optimize the handler by moving initialization outside the handler function
Increase memory allocation to improve CPU and initialization speed
Use Lambda SnapStart for Java functions to reduce cold starts
prevention: Implement a warming function that pings endpoints at regular intervals
Keep function packages under 50MB by using layers and removing unused dependencies
Use Graviton2 (arm64) architecture for better price-performance ratio
Set up CloudWatch alarms for cold start duration > 3 seconds

title: EC2 Spot instances terminated unexpectedly causing workload disruption
symptoms: Kubernetes nodes disappear from the cluster without graceful drain
Application pods rescheduled suddenly causing service degradation
CloudTrail shows ec2:TerminateInstances from AWS account
Spot instance interruption notice not handled properly
root_cause: Spot instances reclaimed by AWS when demand exceeds supply
No instance termination notice handling in the node bootstrap script
Spot instance price bid too low relative to current market
Workload not designed for fault-tolerant spot instance usage
diagnosis: Check EC2 console for Spot instance request status
Review Spot instance interruption notices in instance metadata: `curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/spot/instance-action`
Examine CloudWatch logs for termination signals
Verify Spot instance price history in the console
fix: Implement Spot instance interruption notice handler in user data:

```bash
while true; do
  if [ $(curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/spot/instance-action) ]; then
    kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data $HOSTNAME
    kubectl delete node $HOSTNAME
  fi
  sleep 5
done
```
Use EC2 Auto Scaling groups with mixed instance types
Enable Spot instance diversification across multiple instance pools
Implement graceful shutdown in applications to handle SIGTERM
prevention: Use Capacity Optimized allocation strategy for better Spot availability
Combine Spot with On-Demand or Reserved instances for base capacity
Set up fallback to On-Demand instances when Spot capacity unavailable
Monitor Spot instance interruption rate and adjust strategy accordingly

title: CloudFront returning 504 Gateway Timeout from origin
symptoms: Users intermittently receive 504 errors from CloudFront URLs
CloudFront logs show origin response timeout
Origin server appears healthy when accessed directly
Issue worse during high traffic periods
root_cause: Origin response time exceeds CloudFront default timeout of 30 seconds
Origin server under-provisioned for the incoming request volume
Database queries at origin layer running slowly
Network latency between CloudFront edge and origin server
diagnosis: Check CloudFront logs for 504 errors and origin response times
Access origin server directly to measure response time
Review CloudWatch metrics for origin server CPU and memory
Use trace tools to identify slow downstream dependencies
fix: Increase origin timeout in CloudFront distribution settings to 60 seconds via console or CLI
Optimize origin server performance with caching and database indexing
Implement Lambda@Edge for caching dynamic responses
Scale origin infrastructure horizontally to handle increased load
prevention: Set up CloudWatch alarms for 504 error rate > 1%
Implement origin shielding for high-traffic distributions
Use origin failover with secondary origin for high availability
Regularly load test origin capacity

title: EKS worker nodes stuck in NotReady state after cluster upgrade
symptoms: kubectl get nodes shows nodes in NotReady status
Pods cannot be scheduled on new nodes
Node readiness probe failing or kubelet not responding
Cluster upgrade or node group replacement triggered the issue
root_cause: Outdated kubelet version incompatible with control plane
Missing or incorrect IAM permissions for node IAM role
Security group blocking communication between nodes and control plane
VPC CNI plugin not properly installed or configured
diagnosis: Check node status and conditions: kubectl describe node <node-name>
Review kubelet logs on the node: journalctl -u kubelet
Verify IAM role has required EKS permissions
Check security group rules for allowed ports (1025-65535 for VPC CNI)
fix: Update the kubelet version to match EKS control plane:

```bash
# On the node
sudo /etc/eks/bootstrap.sh <cluster-name> --b64-cluster-ca <ca-cert> --apiserver-endpoint <endpoint>
```
Ensure node IAM role has AmazonEKSWorkerNodePolicy attached
Update security group to allow required traffic:

```bash
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id <sg-id> \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 1025-65535 \
  --source <cidr>
```
Restart kubelet: sudo systemctl restart kubelet
prevention: Use EKS managed node groups for automatic version management
Implement node readiness checks in CI/CD pipeline
Set up CloudWatch alarms for NotReady nodes
Test node upgrades in staging environment before production

title: SQS Dead Letter Queue filling up with unprocessed messages
symptoms: DLQ message count increasing steadily
Processing backlog growing in main queue
Applications not receiving expected messages
CloudWatch alarm triggered for DLQ depth
root_cause: Worker function consistently failing to process messages
Message format changed breaking consumer logic
Processing timeout too short for complex operations
Poison pill messages causing repeated failures
diagnosis: Check SQS metrics for NumberOfMessagesReceived vs Deleted
Review CloudWatch Logs for consumer function errors
Sample messages from DLQ to inspect content
Check receive count on messages to identify retry patterns
fix: Inspect and redrive messages from DLQ after fixing consumer:

```bash
aws sqs start-message-move-task \
  --source-arn <dlq-arn> \
  --destination-arn <main-queue-arn> \
  --max-number-of-messages-per-second 10
```
Increase visibility timeout to match processing time
Add message validation and error handling in consumer
Implement dead letter queue monitoring and alerting
prevention: Set up alarm on DLQ message count > threshold
Implement circuit breaker pattern for failing consumers
Add comprehensive logging for message processing failures
Regularly test message format compatibility with consumers

title: RDS database experiencing slow query performance degrading application
symptoms: Application API response times increased significantly
Database CPU utilization consistently high
Performance Insights showing top SQL queries with high latency
Users reporting timeouts on data-heavy operations
root_cause: Missing or inefficient database indexes on frequently queried columns
Full table scans on large tables instead of index seeks
N+1 query patterns in application code
Database instance class under-provisioned for workload
diagnosis: Enable and review slow query log in RDS parameter group
Use Performance Insights to identify top resource-consuming queries
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on problematic queries
Check CloudWatch metrics for CPU, memory, and IOPS utilization
fix: Add appropriate indexes to frequently queried columns:
Optimize queries by selecting only needed columns
Refactor application code to use JOINs instead of N+1 queries
Scale up database instance class or enable read replicas
prevention: Enable Performance Insights and review regularly
Implement query review process in code reviews
Set up CloudWatch alarms for database CPU > 80%
Use read replicas for read-heavy workloads

title: API Gateway returning 429 Too Many Requests during traffic spikes
symptoms: Clients receiving 429 error responses from API endpoints
Spike in 429 errors correlates with marketing campaigns or events
Legitimate traffic being rejected causing user frustration
CloudWatch metrics showing throttled requests
root_cause: API Gateway default account-level and method-level limits too low
Burst capacity exhausted during sudden traffic increases
No usage plan configured for rate limiting and quotas
Downstream services cannot handle the request rate
diagnosis: Check CloudWatch metrics for Count, 4XXError, and 5XXError
Review API Gateway dashboard for throttle metrics
Inspect usage plan settings if configured
Analyze request patterns to identify legitimate vs abusive traffic
fix: Request service quota increase for API Gateway:

```bash
aws service-quotas request-service-quota-increase \
  --service-code apigateway \
  --quota-code L-xxxxx \
  --desired-value 10000
```
Configure usage plan with appropriate rate and burst limits through the AWS console or CLI
Implement caching at API Gateway level to reduce backend load
Add exponential backoff retry logic in client applications
prevention: Set up CloudWatch alarms for 429 error rate > threshold
Load test API endpoints before major events
Implement caching strategies for frequently accessed data
Use AWS WAF to filter abusive traffic patterns

title: DynamoDB operations throttled due to exceeded capacity
symptoms: Application receiving ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors
Read and write operations failing intermittently
CloudWatch metrics showing throttled requests
Performance degradation during peak usage hours
root_cause: Provisioned throughput insufficient for actual workload
Hot partition issue where access pattern not evenly distributed
Burst traffic exceeding capacity limits
Incorrect capacity estimation during table design
diagnosis: Check CloudWatch metrics for ConsumedReadCapacityUnits and ThrottledRequests
Review DynamoDB console for capacity utilization graphs
Analyze access patterns for partition key distribution
Examine error logs for throttling patterns
fix: Switch to on-demand capacity mode for unpredictable workloads:

```bash
aws dynamodb update-table \
  --table-name my-table \
  --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST
```
Increase provisioned capacity for consistent workloads:

```bash
aws dynamodb update-table \
  --table-name my-table \
  --provisioned-throughput ReadCapacityUnits=100,WriteCapacityUnits=50
```
Implement exponential backoff in application retries
Use DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for read-heavy workloads
prevention: Use on-demand mode for unpredictable or spiky workloads
Design partition keys for even access distribution
Implement auto-scaling for provisioned capacity
Set up CloudWatch alarms for throttled requests
